title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two sociologists, a man and a woman, discussing the psychological effects of noise pollution in cities.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I was reading that new study on 'urban irritability'. It seems the constant hum of traffic is doing more than just keeping us awake; it’s actually eroding social trust. Woman: [1]I’m not surprised. When you're constantly on edge due to auditory overstimulation, you're far less likely to engage in small talk with a neighbor or help a stranger[/1]. Man: Exactly. It’s like we develop this protective 'shell' of indifference just to cope. Woman: [2]The real issue is that planners treat noise as a nuisance rather than a health hazard[/2]. They'll put up a few trees and call it 'mitigation'. Man: It needs a much more radical approach—pedestrianization on a scale we haven't seen yet. Woman: I agree, but try selling that to the delivery logistics lobby.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I was reading that new study on 'urban irritability'. It seems the constant hum of traffic is doing more than just keeping us awake; it’s actually eroding social trust.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I’m not surprised. When you're constantly on edge due to auditory overstimulation, you're far less likely to engage in small talk with a neighbor or help a stranger.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Exactly. It’s like we develop this protective 'shell' of indifference just to cope.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: The real issue is that planners treat noise as a nuisance rather than a health hazard. They'll put up a few trees and call it 'mitigation'.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: It needs a much more radical approach—pedestrianization on a scale we haven't seen yet.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I agree, but try selling that to the delivery logistics lobby.

questions.question: The woman believes that excessive city noise leads to...
questions.explanation: She mentions that people are 'far less likely to... help a stranger', which points to a decline in cooperative/civic behavior.

questions.question: What is the woman's criticism of urban planners?

options.B: They fail to recognize the severity of the problem.
options.C: They are too influenced by the transport industry.

questions.explanation: She states they treat noise as a 'nuisance' (minor annoyance) rather than a 'health hazard', showing they underestimate its severity.

extracts.context: You hear two friends discussing a new investment app that uses game-like features.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: I’ve just downloaded that 'QuestInvest' app. It’s actually making finance fun for once! Man: [3]I’m a bit wary of that whole 'gamification' of the stock market[/3]. It encourages people to treat serious financial decisions like they’re playing a video game. Woman: But it makes it so much more accessible! I’ve learned more about ETFs in three days than I did in three years of reading newspapers. Man: [4]The danger is that it trivializes the risks involved[/4]. The confetti and digital trophies you get for making a trade don't exactly scream 'caution'. Woman: I think you’re overreacting. It’s just a modern way of engaging a younger audience. Man: I hope so, but I fear we’re going to see a lot of people losing money they can't afford to lose because it felt like a game.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I’ve just downloaded that 'QuestInvest' app. It’s actually making finance fun for once!

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’m a bit wary of that whole 'gamification' of the stock market. It encourages people to treat serious financial decisions like they’re playing a video game.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: But it makes it so much more accessible! I’ve learned more about ETFs in three days than I did in three years of reading newspapers.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: The danger is that it trivializes the risks involved. The confetti and digital trophies you get for making a trade don't exactly scream 'caution'.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I think you’re overreacting. It’s just a modern way of engaging a younger audience.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I hope so, but I fear we’re going to see a lot of people losing money they can't afford to lose because it felt like a game.

questions.question: The man expresses skepticism about the app because it...

options.B: promotes an overly casual attitude toward money.
options.C: is designed only for expert investors.

questions.explanation: He says it encourages people to treat 'serious financial decisions' like a 'video game', implying a casual attitude.

questions.question: What specific concern does the man have about the app's interface?

options.A: It obscures the actual cost of trading fees.
options.B: It uses visual rewards to mask potential dangers.
options.C: It is too complex for the average user to navigate.

questions.explanation: He mentions 'confetti and digital trophies' as things that 'trivialize the risks', masking the danger.

extracts.context: You hear two friends, Connor and Emily, talking about the trend of 'remaking' old films and TV shows.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I see they’re rebooting that 90s sci-fi series again. Does Hollywood have any original ideas left? Woman: [5]I think it’s less about a lack of creativity and more about risk aversion[/5]. Studios would rather bet on a known 'brand' than something unproven. Man: It’s a shame. Part of the joy of cinema is being surprised. Woman: True, but [6]there’s also a deep-seated comfort in seeing familiar characters reimagined[/6]. Especially with the state of the world right now, nostalgia is a powerful drug. Man: I get the appeal, but I find most of these remakes just lack the soul of the originals. Woman: Maybe, but as long as they keep breaking box office records, they aren't going to stop.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I see they’re rebooting that 90s sci-fi series again. Does Hollywood have any original ideas left?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I think it’s less about a lack of creativity and more about risk aversion. Studios would rather bet on a known 'brand' than something unproven.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: It’s a shame. Part of the joy of cinema is being surprised.

dialogue.text: True, but there’s also a deep-seated comfort in seeing familiar characters reimagined. Especially with the state of the world right now, nostalgia is a powerful drug.

questions.question: What does the woman identify as the primary reason for the frequency of reboots?

options.A: A genuine shortage of talented scriptwriters.
options.B: A desire to avoid financial uncertainty.
options.C: A demand from audiences for longer storylines.

questions.explanation: She mentions 'risk aversion' and betting on a 'known brand' rather than 'something unproven', which is about financial safety.

questions.question: According to the woman, why are audiences drawn to these remakes?

options.A: They offer a sense of emotional security.
options.B: They are technically superior to the originals.
options.C: They provide a more diverse range of perspectives.

questions.explanation: She speaks of 'deep-seated comfort' and 'nostalgia', which are forms of emotional security/well-being.
